TextENGNew: ``Heeey, welcome, welcome!@k@vS09/00/yatafA17002. Care for one of our special frankfurters?``
changeReason: Old translates フランクフルト as 'hot dog'; new keeps 'frankfurters,' which is the specific item referenced in JPN. Old also drops the elongated, enthusiastic greeting style.

TextENGNew: ``Coming right up!@k@vS09/00/yataiA17002. We've got strawberry, melon, and Blue Hawaii syrup!``
changeReason: Old fabricates 'Our shaved ice is the best!'; Japanese says 'Coming right up!'

TextENGNew: ``Fortune takoyaki, how about it!@k@vS09/00/yataiB17002. If you get the lucky one, it's packed full of mustard!``
changeReason: Old translates おみくじたこ焼き as 'takoyaki roulette'; new uses 'Fortune takoyaki.' Also 'The winner's packed' vs 'If you get the lucky one, it's packed' -- new is closer to JPN アタリ (lucky hit).

TextENGNew: As always, the Watanagashi festival was lined with rows upon rows of food stalls.
Ooishi had always thought that all you needed for a proper festival was Bon dance music and a row of vendors.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: Even though it was only June, the sweltering heat of the past few days had been practically midsummer.
With this weather and Bon dance tunes drifting through a festival, you might as well throw in a fireworks display and call it a real summer celebration.
``This is Kumagai.@k@vS09/19/173100003. Ooishi-san, come in. Ooishi-san, come in.``
A scratchy voice crackled through the earpiece of Ooishi's portable radio.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ...Wearing this earpiece, Ooishi felt like he must look as though he were sneaking a listen to a horse race broadcast while on duty.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ``Yes, this is Ooishi.@k@vS09/11/170700002. Reading you loud and clear.``
``Uh, the town council members have brought us a ton of food.@k@vS09/19/173100005. ...Oden, yakisoba, stewed offal, and... um, some barley tea, too.``
changeReason: Old translates モツ煮込み as 'pork belly stew'; new correctly uses 'stewed offal' (モツ = offal/innards, not pork belly).

TextENGNew: ``Nah ha ha!@k@vS09/11/170700004. That barley tea—is it the kind you can drink on duty,@k@vS09/11/170700005. or the kind you can't?``
``Uhhh, well... ...ah ha ha ha.``
``If the barley tea's in a can, go ahead and accept it.@k@vS09/11/170700007. If it's in a cup, politely decline.``
changeReason: Old has Ooishi accepting the drink himself; new has him instructing Kumagai to accept/decline on his behalf, which better matches the radio conversation context.

TextENGNew: ``Roger that.@k@vS09/19/173100008. ...Oh, and the town council officials are here to pay their respects.@k@vS09/19/173100009. The village chief, and someone from the Sonozaki family, too...``
changeReason: Old says 'the head of the Sonozaki family'; new says 'someone from the Sonozaki family,' which is closer to JPN 園崎家の方からも ('from the Sonozaki side'). Also 'say hi' vs 'pay their respects' for 挨拶.

TextENGNew: ``...Old Lady Oryou?@k@vS09/11/170700009. Oh, come on. Can't you just tell her I'm out on patrol and you don't know when I'll be back?``
changeReason: Old translates お魎のばあ様 as 'that old bag Oryou' (derogatory); new uses 'Old Lady Oryou' (more neutral). Also old drops Ooishi's reluctant/evasive tone (やだなぁ = 'oh, come on').

TextENGNew: ``...Well, the thing is... she said since we have radios, there's no reason we can't call you right back, and she was really insistent about it...``
changeReason: Old adds content and rephrases; new translates more accurately.

TextENGNew: ``Nah ha ha ha!@k@vS09/11/170700011. That old bat—when she finally goes, it'll definitely be a stroke.@k@vS09/11/170700012. Roger, roger. Tell her with the utmost courtesy—and I do mean courtesy, understood?@k@vS09/11/170700013. —that I'll be right back.``
changeReason: New adds '—and I do mean courtesy, understood?' capturing 丁重にですよ？ (emphasis on 'politely, got it?') which old simplifies.

TextENGNew: ...Well, nothing for it.@k Guess it's time to head back and do the community relations thing.
Just then, a pack of kids came barreling past from behind and nearly knocked him off his feet.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: When he looked, it turned out to be... that very old bat's granddaughter and her group of friends.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: They claimed a spot at the shooting gallery and immediately began squealing with excitement.
Mion Sonozaki, huh.
changeReason: New adds 'huh' (か in JPN 園崎魅音か), capturing the reflective/musing quality of the original.

TextENGNew: ...When she acts her age, she's actually fairly cute.
But the moment she puts on her Sonozaki family face, she instantly becomes insufferable.
Give that girl another ten or twenty years, and she'll no doubt be strutting around as a big shot who parts crowds wherever she walks.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ...Or so he'd thought.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: Watching them more closely, the one running the show wasn't Mion—it was the boy.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: Let's see, what was his name... the kid who just moved here recently.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ...Right, the young master of the Maebara estate.@k Keiichi Maebara-kun, if he recalled correctly.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: City folk, and apparently quite well-off.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ...Yet despite that, word was he never lorded it over anyone—a natural humility that had earned him a good reputation.
He hadn't been here long at all, yet he'd already settled right into school life and was apparently quite popular.
His reputation in the village was remarkably good for someone who'd arrived so recently.
Watching him, everything about the boy was exaggerated—every joy, every anger, every sorrow—and he seemed like an entertaining kid.
changeReason: Old summarizes as 'person of many moods'; new translates 喜怒哀楽がいちいちオーバー (every emotion is exaggerated) more faithfully, capturing the specificity of the JPN.

TextENGNew: Boys that age, energy is what matters most.@k It almost made Ooishi nostalgic for his own boyhood.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ``Hey, you're up next, Rena!!@k@vS09/01/170100465. This is a great opening for you!``
changeReason: Old says 'You're lucky it's your turn'; JPN おいしいタイミング means 'a delicious/favorable timing,' which new captures better as 'a great opening.'

TextENGNew: ``Are you okay, Rena?@k@vS09/03/170300235. You're not feeling sick, are you?``
``...Ahaha, sorry.@k@vS09/02/170200589. I think I'll just watch the shooting from the side for now.``
``...Then don't push yourself. Just rest.``
``Well then!@k@vS09/04/170400098. Skipping right over Rena-san, the headliner finally takes the stage!@k@vS09/04/170400099. It's my turn!!``
changeReason: Old omits 真打 (headliner/star performer) and the reference to skipping Rena; new includes both, matching the JPN content.

TextENGNew: ...Looking more closely, only four of them were caught up in the excitement. The girl called Rena seemed to be standing apart from it all.
Ryuugu Rena, that was her name.
What was her real name again...?
He could look it up when he got back, but he couldn't recall it now.@k At any rate, Rena was just a nickname.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: She'd moved here about a year before Maebara-kun, if he remembered right.
He seemed to recall hearing that her mother had left—a divorce.
And she handled all the housework herself, which apparently made her quite an admirable young lady.
On top of that, he'd heard she was remarkably well-mannered for a girl her age—a rarity these days.
Still, compared to Maebara-kun—another transfer student—she didn't seem to have fully opened up to the others yet.
The five of them appeared to be together, but Rena alone couldn't seem to join in the revelry, lingering at the edge of the group looking dejected.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: From an outsider's perspective, Maebara-kun had already blended in completely as a villager, and yet... she alone seemed left behind.@k ...That was how it looked to Ooishi, at least.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ``Ooishi-san, the station chief and the section manager are here.@k@vS09/19/173100013. Please come back right away!``

"Ooishi, roger!@k@vS09/11/170700015. ......He comes all the way to a local festival just to pay his respects—our station chief sure is diligent about community relations.@k@vS09/11/170700016. Funny, I've never heard of him attending the Okinomiya festival."
changeReason: New translates additional Japanese content about the Okinomiya festival that old omits entirely.

TextENGNew: ``Aah, this is Takasugi.@k@vS09/00/taka17002. Ooishi-san, can you hear me? Come in, come in!``
``Yes, yes, this is Ooishi.@k@vS09/11/170700018. I'm currently heading back at top speed.@k@vS09/11/170700019. Give me about one more minute.``
changeReason: Old says 'I hear you'; JPN says 大石です (this is Ooishi). Also 'I'm on my way back right now' vs 'I'm currently heading back at top speed' -- new includes 全速力 (top speed).

TextENGNew: If he didn't get back soon, the station chief himself might get on the radio next.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: Ooishi broke into a jog and headed back toward the command tent.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ``Ooishi-san, the doctor has arrived.``
changeReason: Old adds 'Dr. Irie' where Japanese says generic '先生'; new keeps it as 'the doctor.'

TextENGNew: ``Ah, much appreciated!@k@vS09/11/170700021. How does it look, Dr. Irie?``
Ooishi stepped into the area cordoned off by blue tarps.
changeReason: New includes ブルーシート (blue tarps/sheets) which old omits.

TextENGNew: Inside, forensics personnel were photographing the body from every conceivable angle.
Irie, who had been crouching beside the body of Jirou Tomitake, rose to his feet.@k ...His face was deathly pale.
changeReason: Old says 'he looked pale'; new says 'deathly pale' (蒼白 = ashen/deathly pale), conveying stronger severity.

TextENGNew: ``...I can't believe this.``
``...Well, I can hardly believe it myself.@k@vS09/11/170700023. From what I can see at first glance... the deceased clawed his own throat apart.@k@vS09/11/170700024. With his bare hands, no less.``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quotes and says 'clawed out his throat'; new says 'clawed his own throat apart' + adds 'with his bare hands, no less' emphasis. New also adds 'at first glance' for 所見.

TextENGNew: ``This is no ordinary way to die.@k@vS09/11/170700026. Nine times out of ten, it's some kind of nasty drug.``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening double-backtick quote mark. New correctly includes it. Also 'Most likely' vs 'Nine times out of ten' (十中八九) which new translates more precisely.

TextENGNew: ``...Are you suggesting he was some kind of habitual drug user?``
``Who knows?``
changeReason: Old is missing the closing quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``That's what we'll have to find out, I suppose.``
changeReason: Old says 'my job' but Japanese says 我々 (our job).

TextENGNew: ``...Ooishi-san.@k@vS09/10/171000032. What about this piece of lumber?``
changeReason: Old translates 角材 as 'two-by-four' (a specific American lumber dimension); new uses 'piece of lumber,' which is more accurate for the generic JPN term.

TextENGNew: ``Probably... the deceased was swinging it around, I'd guess.@k@vS09/11/170700030. Then at some point he dropped it and found clawing at his throat more... satisfying.``
``...What would he have been swinging lumber around for?``
``Self-defense would be the most natural assumption.``
changeReason: Old is missing the closing quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``...Have you noticed the external injuries on the body?``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``...Yes. I can see what appear to be several contusions scattered about.``
changeReason: Old simplifies to 'some bruises'; new translates 打撲傷を思わせる痕がいくつか散見出来ます more fully ('several contusions scattered about').

TextENGNew: ``Which would mean the most likely scenario is... the deceased was attacked by someone here on his way back to Okinomiya after the festival.``
changeReason: Old is missing the closing quote mark. Also 'easiest explanation' vs 'most likely scenario' for 一番考えられるストーリー.

TextENGNew: ``He was seized, injected with some suspicious substance or the like, and as a result was driven to a frenzy that killed him.``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``...I've never heard of any drug that could compel someone to claw out their own throat.``
``I'm no specialist in that area either, but... self-harm is a known symptom of certain drug reactions, isn't it?@k@vS09/11/170700036. Well, our old man in forensics will sort that part out.``
``Pardon the interruption, Ooishi-san!@k@vS09/19/173100015. A bicycle believed to belong to Jirou Tomitake has been found several hundred meters from here.``
changeReason: Old says 'a hundred meters' but Japanese says 数百メートル (several hundred meters).

TextENGNew: ``Oh, is that so? Much appreciated.@k@vS09/11/170700038. Any leads with it?``
``Nothing notable at first glance.@k@vS09/19/173100017. Forensics is on it now.``
changeReason: Old says 'Not yet' (implying ongoing work); JPN 所見では特には means 'nothing particular at first glance.' New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: ``Then I suppose we can assume... the location of that bicycle is where the attack took place.``
changeReason: Old is missing the closing quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``...And from there, he fought and fled until he ended up here... ...hmm.``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote. Also 'ran away from the suspect' vs 'fought and fled' (戦ったり逃げたり); new correctly includes both fighting and fleeing.

TextENGNew: Ooishi folded his arms and let out a low grunt.
changeReason: Old says 'frowned' but Japanese 唸る means 'grunted' -- different physical action.

TextENGNew: Irie stared at the impossible horror of Tomitake's corpse and kept repeating the same words.
``...It's impossible.@k@vS09/10/171000037. ...Absolutely impossible.``

``...Doctor, it happened, so clearly it was possible.@k@vS09/11/170700042. Let's try to be flexible in our thinking.@k@vS09/11/170700043_1. ...Well, I suppose even a doctor doesn't see something like this every day...``

``...You've done enough, Doctor.@k@vS09/11/170700045. Please go home and get some rest.@k@vS09/11/170700046. We'd called you for emergency medical assistance, but it turned out to be a wasted trip. My apologies.``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening quote mark. Also old starts with 'Anyway, thanks for your cooperation'; JPN starts with もう結構ですよ (you've done enough/that's enough).

TextENGNew: Ooishi poked his head outside the tarp enclosure and called out, asking if someone could give Dr. Irie a ride home.
changeReason: Old says 'blue tent'; JPN says 囲みの外 (outside the enclosure). Also old uses direct quote; new uses indirect narration matching JPN style.

TextENGNew: At that moment, Irie suddenly started and stepped closer to Ooishi.
``Wait... what about Takano-san?@k@vS09/10/171000039. What happened to Takano-san?!``
``I believe she was with Tomitake-san.@k@vS09/10/171000041. Is Takano-san safe?!``
changeReason: Old uses pronoun 'him'; new uses 'Tomitake-san' matching JPN 富竹さん. Also 'Is she okay?!' vs 'Is Takano-san safe?!' -- new uses name matching JPN.

TextENGNew: ``...Doctor, do you happen to know her phone number?``
``Of course I do.``
``Kuma-chan.@k@vS09/11/170700051. Get her phone number from the doctor and have the station confirm whether she's home.``
``Yes sir!@k@vS09/19/173100019. Doctor, the number, please.``
changeReason: Old says 'Understood'; JPN 了解っす is casual/deferential, new's 'Yes sir!' better captures the subordinate tone.

TextENGNew: ``Yes, um, are you ready?``
changeReason: Old says 'here it is' (providing number); JPN よろしいですか means 'are you ready?' (asking if they can write it down). New is accurate.

TextENGNew: Kumagai took the number from Irie, repeated it back to confirm, then dashed out past the blue tarps.
``Doctor.@k@vS09/11/170700053. ...Takano-san and the deceased were quite close, weren't they?``
changeReason: Old says 'Tomitake-san' (name); new says 'the deceased' (ホトケ = Buddhist term for deceased), matching the police jargon used in JPN.

TextENGNew: ``...Have you ever heard of any trouble between them?``
``Absolutely not.@k@vS09/10/171000046. ...Never once.@k@vS09/10/171000047. They were on very good terms.``

``Well, let's hope nothing's happened to her.@k@vS09/11/170700056. ...Worst case, she's another victim—or she could be the perpetrator.@k@vS09/11/170700057. ...Hmm, come to think of it, a nurse would have ready access to dubious substances, wouldn't she?``
``...Our clinic doesn't stock anything like that, and even if it did, it would be kept under strict lock and key.@k@vS09/10/171000049. There's no way Takano-san could have taken anything out...``
``Even so, it would be easier for her than for an ordinary person, wouldn't it?``
``As I keep saying, I've never even heard of such a drug, and nothing of the sort exists at our clinic...!``

``Ooishi-san, it seems she's not home.@k@vS09/19/173100021. The standby team at the station says they'll go check in person.@k@vS09/19/173100022. Doctor, if you don't mind, could you give us her address?``

``Kuma-chan.@k@vS09/11/170700061. On top of that, check with prefectural headquarters whether any unidentified female bodies have turned up tonight.@k@vS09/11/170700062. ...It's possible she could be outside the prefecture, too.``
changeReason: Old is missing the closing quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``Tell the section manager to check with neighboring prefectures as well, see if any unidentified female bodies have turned up.``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``Yes sir!``
``...Are you saying that Takano-san was... murdered as well...?!``
changeReason: Old says 'might be dead' but Japanese 殺されている means 'was murdered' -- different implication.

TextENGNew: ``Well now, n-fu-fu, who can say.@k@vS09/11/170700065. We haven't determined yet whether she's a victim or a suspect.``
changeReason: Old is missing the closing quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``Of course, it's also entirely possible she's unrelated.@k@vS09/11/170700067. Tomorrow's Monday, but she could be out drinking at some bar without a care in the world.``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``...This is unbelievable...@k@vS09/10/171000052. How could something like this happen...?``
changeReason: Old asks 'Who does things like this?' (asking about perpetrator); JPN どうしてこんなことが means 'How could something like this happen?' (questioning the event itself). New is accurate.

TextENGNew: ``Well, I find it unbelievable too.@k@vS09/11/170700069. The Hinamizawa serial mysterious death cases.@k@vS09/11/170700070. ...This makes five years running.@k@vS09/11/170700071. Five consecutive curses of Oyashiro-sama.``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote. Also old paraphrases; new translates the proper name 雛見沢村連続怪死事件 (Hinamizawa Village Serial Mysterious Death Cases).

TextENGNew: ``...Damn it. I want to grab them by the tail this year, no matter what...!@k@vS09/11/170700073. I need to close this case before I retire.``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote. Also old says 'get whoever's behind it'; JPN シッポを掴む means 'grab the tail' (catch evidence/leads). New preserves the idiom.

TextENGNew: Ooishi seethed with loathing for 'the curse of Oyashiro-sama' that had toyed with him for five straight years.
...To Ooishi, the first year's victim—the construction site foreman—had been a friend.
changeReason: Old says 'manager'; Japanese 現場監督 means 'foreman.' Also perspective correction.

TextENGNew: Though he longed to avenge that friend, the mystery only deepened with each passing year, and new cases piled up as if to mock him.
What's more, Ooishi would be reaching mandatory retirement age next March.
changeReason: Old says 'retiring'; new specifies 'mandatory retirement age' (定年), distinguishing between voluntary retirement and reaching the mandatory age limit.

TextENGNew: ...As a retired officer, he might still be able to inquire about the progress of the investigation, but he would no longer be able to lead it himself.
In other words... this year was Ooishi's last chance to avenge his friend.
...Was there any choice but to venture into the tiger's den?
changeReason: Old uses 'lion's mouth'; JPN 虎穴 means 'tiger's den' (from the proverb 虎穴に入らずんば虎子を得ず). New preserves the correct animal and cultural reference.

TextENGNew: Ooishi quietly clenched his fist where no one could see.
I believe.
I believe this labyrinth has an exit.
changeReason: Old uses 'maze'; new uses 'labyrinth' for 迷路. While both are valid, the subsequent philosophical discussion uses 'labyrinth' consistently in the new translation.

TextENGNew: But the word 'labyrinth' is a terribly convenient one.
Because a labyrinth, by definition, always has an exit.
Something without an exit isn't called a labyrinth.
This time, I'm navigating the small forks I usually overlook without making a single wrong turn.
changeReason: Old adds substantial content not in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: This time feels different from all the others.
Surely, this time, I can finally reach the exit of this labyrinth.
But... no matter which path I choose to arrive at this point, as if every road inevitably leads back here... Jirou Tomitake dies once more.
Rena had been feeling unwell since that day and had often been absent from club activities.
``...Yeah.@k@vS09/02/170200591. I still have a bit of a cold.``
changeReason: Old says 'feverish'; JPN 風邪っぽい means 'cold-like/having a cold.' New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: ``...Is that so...@k@vS09/04/170400101. What a shame...``

``Hey, Rena!@k@vS09/01/170100467. Why don't you just cut loose and have some fun for once?@k@vS09/01/170100468. I bet if you just played with us and got your energy going, it'd blow this whole thing away!``
changeReason: Old says 'just try and play with us today'; new captures ぱーっとはしゃぐ (cut loose and have fun) more vividly. Also new includes the narrator's personal opinion (俺は) at the end.

TextENGNew: ``Yeah, yeah, this old man here agrees with that.``
``...You think so? You think?``
``...Sometimes lying around doing nothing only makes it worse.``
``Quite right.@k@vS09/04/170400103. They do say illness begins in the mind, you know.``
Everyone had vaguely sensed that what ailed Rena wasn't a cold in the true sense.
Too many things had happened at once, and the mental shock had simply given her a cold of the heart.
That's why we thought if she could just immerse herself in the same carefree chaos we used to share, her spirits would surely recover in no time.
Rena seemed to understand that, too.
She must have known she couldn't stay like this forever—that she needed to pick herself up and reclaim those joyful days.
``...All right!@k@vS09/02/170200594. I'll join in too!``
``All right!@k@vS09/03/170300238. That's what I like to hear!``
``Okay, since Rena's still recovering, let's pick something from the board games today.``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening quote mark, presenting dialogue as narration.

TextENGNew: ``...What should we play, what should we play...``
changeReason: Old is missing the opening quote mark.

TextENGNew: Mion began rummaging through her fourth-dimensional locker, humming as she went.
changeReason: Old omits 四次元 (fourth-dimensional) humorous descriptor.

TextENGNew: Not this one, not that one—she pulled game after game from inside and stacked them to the side.
That locker clearly defied the laws of physics, though. There was no other explanation.
The pile of games she'd pulled out plainly exceeded anything that locker should have been able to hold.
``Oh, look what we've got here.@k@vS09/03/170300242. It's been a while—let's try this one.``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``Today we're going with a real deduction game!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``So, it's a game where you have to figure out the culprit, the murder weapon, and the scene of the crime.@k@vS09/03/170300245. It takes some real brainpower.``
``...You'll pick it up once you start playing.@k@vS09/05/170500078. As long as you keep organized notes, you'll be fine.``
``Oh ho ho!@k@vS09/04/170400105. There's a certain trick to organizing information, you know!@k@vS09/04/170400106. This is precisely the genre where my intellect truly shines!``
changeReason: Old paraphrases loosely; new translates more accurately about organizing information.

TextENGNew: Once he heard the rules, they didn't seem all that difficult.
In short, it was a game where you probed each other's hands to deduce the identity of the cards that had been drawn and set aside face-down at the start.
The format differed, but games where you try to identify something hidden with as few moves as possible were common enough.@k This one just put it into a competitive format.
The key to victory would lie in how well you could organize your information.
...Normally they all loved to run around and make a ruckus, yet they could handle a game like this just as easily—a reminder that the club members were truly versatile in every way.
changeReason: Old completely rewrites the Japanese content about the club's versatility.

TextENGNew: ``All right, all right, now this is getting interesting!!``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``Let's go, let's go!!@k@vS09/01/170100474. Rena, today I'm settling the score from that water gun match once and for all!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote mark.

TextENGNew: ``Is that so?``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote mark. JPN そうだね can mean 'is that so' or 'that's right' -- context-dependent.

TextENGNew: ``Well then, Rena's going all out too!!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote mark. Also old says 'I'm not going to go easy on you'; JPN says レナも本気で行く (Rena is going all out too).

TextENGNew: ``...If Rena is getting serious, then I'll have to get serious too.``
``Indeed!@k@vS09/04/170400108. Ever since that water gun incident, I've had to revise my opinion of Keiichi-san and Rena-san.@k@vS09/04/170400109. I shall show no mercy!!``
``Ah ha ha ha!@k@vS09/03/170300247. Now that's the spirit!!@k@vS09/03/170300248. This old man was hoping for exactly this kind of fire!@k@vS09/03/170300249. Come on, let's get started!!``
changeReason: New translates more of the JPN content including よし！行ってみよー！ as a separate line, preserving Mion's complete dialogue. Old truncates the ending.

TextENGNew: ``Here we go!``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote. JPN よし行くぜ means 'All right, let's go!' New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: ``I suggest it was 'Me' with the 'Knife' in the 'Study'!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote. Also old says 'Library'; JPN 書斎 means 'study/den.' New is correct.

TextENGNew: ``Got one!``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote.

TextENGNew: ``Rena's turn! It was 'Keiichi-kun' with 'Poison' in the 'Entrance Hall'!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote. JPN 玄関ホール means 'entrance hall'; old uses just 'Hall.' New is more specific.

TextENGNew: ``My turn! 'Mion' with the 'Pistol' in the 'Lounge'!``
``...Heh heh heh!``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote.

TextENGNew: ``You're the one holding the 'Pistol' card, aren't you?@k@vS09/03/170300253. A clumsy bluff like that won't work!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote.

TextENGNew: ``...I believe I've identified the culprit and the murder weapon.@k@vS09/05/170500081. It's the crime scene that's down to two possibilities.``
``Hau!@k@vS09/02/170200600. You too, Rika-chan?@k@vS09/02/170200601. I've still got the culprit down to two and can't narrow it further!``
changeReason: Old says 'You're good' (compliment) but Japanese means 'You too?' (surprise).

TextENGNew: ``The trick is to glean hints from the questions your opponents ask as well.@k@vS09/04/170400112. Once you understand that, sometimes you can find the answer through their very moves!``
changeReason: Old adds 'I'll give you a tip' not in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ``I-I see!@k@vS09/01/170100478. Damn, I underestimated this!@k@vS09/01/170100479. ...Crap, my notes are such a mess I can't make sense of them anymore!``
``Ahahahaha!@k@vS09/03/170300255. Once you figure out the trick to taking notes, you've already mastered seventy percent of this game.``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote.

TextENGNew: ``Though seventy percent still won't be enough to beat this old man!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote.

TextENGNew: At first it felt like a game where victory was out of reach, but gradually he began to get the hang of it.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: Looking over, Rena seemed to be regaining her usual energy as she played.
It really had been the right call to coax her into joining, after all.
While they were happily playing away, Chie-sensei suddenly appeared.
changeReason: Old uses first-person narration but Japanese uses third-person (Ooishi's perspective).

TextENGNew: ``You all look like you're having fun.@k@vS09/22/171400005. By the way, is Ryuugu-san here?``
``Ryuugu-san has a visitor.``
changeReason: Old says 'You have a visitor' (speaking to Rena directly); JPN 竜宮さんにお客様が来ています uses Ryuugu-san's name, suggesting she's speaking to the group. New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: Everyone blinked in surprise.
...Occasionally a parent would come to school, but when that happened, the teacher never called them a 'visitor.'
So this was truly something unusual.
``...I wonder who it could be.@k@vS09/02/170200604. I'll go see. Be right back.@k@vS09/02/170200605. Everyone, keep playing without me!``
Rena trotted off out of the classroom.
``...What should we do?@k@vS09/01/170100481. Wait for her?``
``Hmm, we don't know how long it'll take.``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote.

TextENGNew: ``Rena did say to keep going.@k@vS09/03/170300259. So let's continue!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote.

TextENGNew: The entrance hall was painted in sharp contrasts of blazing sunlight and cool shadow.
Waiting there amid the heat was a middle-aged man, jacket tucked under one arm, tie loosened sloppily.
changeReason: Old says 'old man'; JPN says 中年のおっさん (middle-aged man). New is correct.

TextENGNew: ``Are you Ryuugu-san?@k@vS09/11/170700075. Reina Ryuugu-san.``
Ever since Rina had called me Reina, I'd come to loathe my real name even more. So right from the start, I didn't have a good impression of this stranger.
``That's right.@k@vS09/02/170200607. ...And you are?``
``My car has air conditioning, so let's talk in there.@k@vS09/11/170700077. Isn't it hot here?``
The man casually ignored my question, pointed toward a car parked at the school gate, and strode off.
...If this were a normal conversation, he would have just used the teachers' office.
The fact that he wanted to talk in the privacy of his own car only deepened my suspicion.
But Chie-sensei had let him through to see me.
Which meant that, as far as she was concerned, this man wasn't suspicious.
...Who on earth was he...?
``I'm not going to eat you.@k@vS09/11/170700079. Please, right this way.``
He held the rear door of the car open, beckoning me inside.
...Could this man be... police...?
Could it be that... my killing Rina and the others... had come to the police's attention...?
changeReason: Old says 'those two'; new specifies 'Rina and the others' matching JPN リナたち. Also different phrasing captures different urgency.

TextENGNew: A wave of dark suspicion surged through my mind.
changeReason: Old translates 疑心暗鬼 as 'anxious'; JPN 疑心暗鬼 specifically means 'dark suspicion/paranoia.' New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: ...Was this what they called being asked to come in 'voluntarily' for questioning?
...I wanted nothing to do with this, but refusing here would only make me look worse.
I steeled myself... and got into the car.
The interior really was cool.
A car air conditioner must be quite the luxury.@k My father's car certainly didn't have one.
``Let me know if it's too cold, all right?@k@vS09/11/170700081. I tend to crank it up.``
``So, what is it you want with me?``
Returning the favor, I ignored his question and cut straight to the point, just as he had done.
The man pulled a small notebook from his breast pocket, flipped through the pages, and extracted a single photograph tucked inside.
In it was the face of a man... with a vacant, drowsy expression.
changeReason: Old translates 寝ぼけたような as 'goofy-looking'; JPN means 'drowsy/half-asleep-looking.' New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: ``If there's anything you know about this man, please tell me.``
The man in the photograph wore a blank, half-asleep look.
An expressionless photo can sometimes be unrecognizable even to family members.
...Let alone to someone unrelated like me.
...Inwardly, I felt a small measure of relief.@k ...This had nothing to do with Rina and the others.
``There were scribbles on his shirt done in marker.@k@vS09/11/170700084. It bore the signatures of several students, including yours, Ryuugu-san.``
changeReason: New includes マジック (marker/Sharpie) which old omits.

TextENGNew: ``...Wait... is this... Tomitake-san?!``
That usual air of his—somewhat unreliable yet breezy—was completely absent from this photograph.@k A drowsy, emotionless face...
``And do you recognize this woman?``
Even before looking, I had a feeling I already knew.
``...That's Miyo-san.@k@vS09/02/170200611. Miyo Takano-san.``
``When was the last time you saw either of them?``
``I think it was just after the Watanagashi festival ended.``
``Did you notice anything unusual?@k@vS09/11/170700088. Anything at all.@k@vS09/11/170700089. Please tell me.``
``Did... something happen to Tomitake-san and the others?``
changeReason: Old uses pronoun 'them'; new specifies 'Tomitake-san and the others' matching JPN 富竹さんたち.
Old is missing closing quote.

TextENGNew: ``To tell you the truth, I was torn between 'Rena' and 'Mion'.``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote.

TextENGNew: ``So it was a gamble?!``
changeReason: Old is missing closing quote.

TextENGNew: ``...What a reckless thing to do...!``
changeReason: Old is missing opening quote.

TextENGNew: ``...A win is still a win.``
changeReason: Old expands to two sentences with added meaning; JPN 勝ちは勝ち simply means 'a win is a win.' New is accurate.

TextENGNew: ``Oh, it's Rena.@k@vS09/03/170300261. Welcome back!@k@vS09/03/170300262. That took a while, huh?``
Mion noticed that Rena had come back.
Rena looked utterly exhausted.
...I had no idea what that conversation had been about, but whatever it was, it clearly hadn't been pleasant.
``Rena... are you okay?``
``I mean your face.@k@vS09/03/170300265. You're either blue or white.@k@vS09/03/170300266. ...Are you really all right?``
changeReason: Old says 'I'm talking about you. You look pale'; new says 'I mean your face. You're either blue or white' matching JPN 顔色だよ。青いというか白いというか (your complexion—blue or white). New is more specific.

TextENGNew: Rena tried to insist she was fine, but her expression darkened for just an instant before she said she'd leave early after all.
...Maybe we shouldn't have pushed her into joining club activities after all.
I felt a pang of regret.@k Rena had probably been forcing herself to go along with us despite not feeling well.
Rena packed her things into her bag, apologized for ducking out early, and left the classroom.
``Well, it can't be helped.@k@vS09/04/170400117. ...Then at the very least, let us settle things amongst ourselves!``
changeReason: Old says 'she's gone now' not in the Japanese; Japanese says 'it can't be helped.'

TextENGNew: ``You're right!@k@vS09/03/170300268. Now, whose turn was it again?``

changeReason: Old translates みー as 'Meep!'; new keeps it as 'Mii!' which is Rika's signature sound.

TextENGNew: ``All right, Rika-chan!@k@vS09/01/170100487. Bring it on!!!``
